  "opinion": "As a retired woman from Lecce who has spent much of her life in the Italian countryside, I find that social media can be both a source of comfort and a place of anxiety. My depression often makes me sensitive to the noise online, especially when I see misinformation that seems to come from trusted sources. I remember growing up in a small town where neighbors would share stories over coffee, and now I worry that some of those stories have been distorted online. My hobbies like photography and painting help me focus on reality, but the constant stream of fake news can make me feel isolated. I’ve learned to limit my time on social media to a few times a month, as my parents taught me to be cautious about what you share. When I do check, I try to verify facts with local news outlets or ask friends who know the truth. It’s important to remember that the world is more complex than what you see on a screen, and sometimes the best way to manage fake content is to step back and reconnect with what really matters—like the quiet moments of nature or the laughter of family. My depression makes me feel vulnerable to the chaos online, so I focus on the simple things: a good cup of coffee, a walk in the park, or a conversation with someone I trust. Social media isn’t the enemy; it’s just a tool that needs to be used with care and awareness.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a retired woman from Lecce who has spent much of her life in the Italian countryside, I find that social media can be both a source of comfort and a place of anxiety. My depression often makes me sensitive to the noise online, especially when I see misinformation that seems to come from trusted sources. I remember growing up in a small town where neighbors would share stories over coffee, and now I worry that some of those stories have been distorted online. My hobbies like photography and painting help me focus on reality, but the constant stream of fake news can make me feel isolated. I’ve learned to limit my time on social media to a few times a month, as my parents taught me to be cautious about what you share. When I do check, I try to verify facts with local news outlets or ask friends who know the truth. It’s important to remember that the world is more complex than what you see on a screen, and sometimes the best way to manage fake content is to step back and reconnect with what really matters—like the quiet moments of nature or the laughter of family. My depression makes me feel vulnerable to the chaos online, so I focus on the simple things: a good cup of coffee, a walk in the park, or a conversation with someone I trust. Social media isn’t the enemy; it’s just a tool that needs to be used with care and awareness.
